title: Ezekiel 46
subtitle: The Prince's Worship, Land Inheritance, and Temple Kitchens

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — New Temple Vision

sections.header: Verses 1-15 — The Prince's Sabbath and Daily Offerings

heb.paragraph: The olah ('that which ascends') is the principal sacrifice prescribed for the prince on Sabbaths and new moons. On Sabbaths, the prince provides six lambs and a ram; on new moons, a bull, six lambs, and a ram. The olah is wholly consumed on the altar, with no portion returned to the offerer, symbolizing complete dedication to YHWH. The quantities exceed those prescribed in Numbers 28:9-10 (two lambs for the Sabbath), reflecting the enhanced scale of the eschatological temple's worship.

The east gate of the inner court opens only on Sabbaths and new moons for the prince's worship. On the six working days it remains shut. This regulated opening creates a liturgical rhythm: the gate's status marks sacred time. The prince stands at the gatepost while the priests officiate, then worships at the threshold. He enters the vestibule from outside but never passes through the gate itself.

refs.note: The Mosaic Sabbath offering was two male lambs. Ezekiel's prescription of six lambs and a ram represents a significant expansion, reflecting the greater glory of the eschatological temple.
The prince's position at the east gate was introduced in 44:3, where he eats bread before YHWH in the gate vestibule. Chapter 46 elaborates his liturgical role at this same location.
The daily tamid offering of two lambs (morning and evening). Ezekiel prescribes only a morning lamb, possibly simplifying the tradition or reflecting a different practice.
Enter His gates with thanksgiving and His courts with praise. The regulated processional flow of 46:9-10 embodies this psalm's vision of ordered, joyful approach to God's presence.

notes.note: The inner east gate opens on Sabbaths and new moons as a liturgical calendar marker. The gate's opening and closing creates a visual rhythm of sacred and ordinary time. The prince stands at the threshold; he approaches God's presence but does not enter the priests' domain. This spatial arrangement embodies the separation of civil and sacerdotal authority.

The Sabbath and new moon offerings are generous, far exceeding the Mosaic prescriptions. The prince provides from his own resources, demonstrating that leadership in worship requires personal sacrifice. The phrase 'as he is able to give' for the lamb grain offering introduces an element of voluntary generosity alongside the fixed requirements.

The processional traffic regulation — entering by one gate and exiting by the opposite — prevents worshipers from turning their backs on the sanctuary. This detail reveals that even the direction of foot traffic has theological significance. The prince goes in among the worshipers, demonstrating solidarity with the people he leads.

The prince's freewill offering is voluntary, unlike the mandated Sabbath and new moon sacrifices. When the prince offers voluntarily, the east gate is opened specially for him, then closed after he leaves. Voluntary worship receives special accommodation.

The daily tamid offering — a year-old lamb every morning — is a perpetual ordinance (chuqqat olam). The continual offering ensures that every day, without exception, begins with sacrifice. The morning sacrifice grounds the nation's daily life in worship.

Calvin emphasizes the regulated opening and closing of the gate as a principle of divine order. God does not leave access to His presence to human discretion but establishes fixed times and modes of approach. The Sabbath and new moon openings teach that worship requires discipline and structure, not mere spontaneous feeling.

Calvin delights in the traffic regulation as a symbol of spiritual progress: the worshiper who enters by one gate and exits by the other has been changed by the encounter. One does not return the same way one came. This reflects the transformative nature of genuine worship.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that the prince worships 'at the threshold of the gate' (miphtan hashshaar), not inside the gate proper. His worship position is liminal — between the outer court (the people's space) and the inner court (the priests' space). This precise spatial language defines the prince's role as a mediator who belongs fully to neither domain.

The NET observes that the daily grain offering prescription includes the phrase 'a perpetual ordinance' (chuqqat olam), the same terminology used in Exodus 29:42 for the tamid offering. The eschatological temple inherits and continues the perpetual worship cycle established at Sinai.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block identifies a chiastic structure in the chapter: Sabbath/new moon regulations (vv. 1-8), processional regulations (vv. 9-10), freewill offering (vv. 11-12), and daily tamid (vv. 13-15). The center of the chiasm is the processional rule, which Block interprets as the theological heart: worship is directional movement toward God that transforms the worshiper. Block also notes that the nasi's role throughout is consistently that of patron and exemplar, never celebrant. He provides, the priests officiate.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli classifies this section as Gottesdienstordnung (worship ordinance) and notes the tension between fixed and voluntary elements. The mandated Sabbath and new moon offerings represent institutional worship; the freewill offering in v. 12 preserves space for spontaneous devotion. Zimmerli identifies the daily tamid prescription (vv. 13-15) as a later addition to the original Sabbath/new moon legislation, reflecting the post-exilic community's standardization of daily worship.

hist.context: The inner east gate remains shut during the six working days but opens on Sabbaths and new moons for the prince's worship. The prince enters the gate vestibule from the outer court and stands at the gatepost while the Zadokite priests offer his burnt offerings and fellowship offerings. He worships at the threshold and then withdraws; the gate remains open until evening. The people also worship at the entrance of the gate on Sabbaths and new moons. The Sabbath offering is six lambs and a ram (with grain offerings of one ephah per ram, and 'as much as he wishes' for the lambs), while the new moon offering adds a young bull. A unique traffic regulation governs festival processions: worshipers who enter by the north gate must exit by the south gate, and vice versa, ensuring a continuous flow and preventing congestion. The daily offering is a year-old lamb each morning with a grain offering of one-sixth ephah and one-third hin of oil. This tamid (continual offering) anchors the daily worship cycle.

sections.header: Verses 16-18 — Inheritance Laws for the Prince

heb.paragraph: The prince's nachalah (inheritance) may be gifted to his sons and remains within the family permanently. If he gifts land to a servant, however, it reverts to the prince in the year of liberty (shenat haddror, the Jubilee). This prevents the permanent alienation of princely territory. Critically, the prince may not seize land from the people's inheritance (nachalat ha-am), preventing the royal land-grabbing that characterized the monarchy (cf. 1 Kgs 21, Naboth's vineyard).

refs.note: The Jubilee legislation requires the return of ancestral land every fifty years. Ezekiel's year of liberty (shenat haddror) adapts this principle for the prince's land grants to servants.
Ahab's seizure of Naboth's vineyard through Jezebel's conspiracy. Ezekiel's prohibition directly addresses this type of royal land-grabbing.
Woe to those who add house to house and field to field until there is no space left. Ezekiel's property law prevents the latifundia system that concentrated land in the hands of the powerful.

notes.note: The distinction between gifts to sons (permanent) and gifts to servants (temporary, reverting at Jubilee) preserves the prince's territorial integrity across generations while preventing servants from accumulating princely land. This is prudent governance that balances generosity with stewardship.

The prince must not take any of the people's inheritance. This is the most explicit anti-tyranny provision in Ezekiel's constitution. Royal power is bounded by the people's property rights. The lesson of Naboth's vineyard has been encoded into the fundamental law of the new Israel.

Calvin reads this as a universal principle of just governance: rulers must not enrich themselves at the expense of their subjects. The prince's wealth must come from his own portion, not from confiscation. Calvin applies this to all civil magistrates: taxation and eminent domain must be exercised with restraint and justice, never for personal enrichment.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that 'year of liberty' (shenat haddror) is the same terminology used in Leviticus 25:10 for the Jubilee year. The use of dror ('release, liberty') connects Ezekiel's prince-legislation to the broader Jubilee tradition of economic reset and social justice.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block identifies this unit as 'constitutional property law' that limits princely power through three provisions: dynastic inheritance is permanent, servant grants are temporary, and expropriation of citizens' land is absolutely prohibited. Block notes that this is the most concrete application of the anti-oppression mandate of 45:8-9. The prince is powerful but constitutionally constrained.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli sees this unit as a Nasi-Ordnung (prince ordinance) that addresses the most dangerous abuse of monarchic power: land confiscation. He notes that the vocabulary of nachalah (inheritance) ties this passage to the broader Deuteronomistic theology of land as divine gift that no human authority may permanently redistribute.

hist.context: This brief unit establishes land-inheritance regulations for the prince. The prince may grant portions of his territory to his sons as a permanent inheritance, maintaining dynastic stability. Gifts to servants, however, revert to the prince in the year of liberty (the Jubilee), preventing permanent alienation of royal land to non-family members. The critical prohibition is negative: the prince may not take any of the people's inheritance, pushing them off their property. This directly addresses the abuse exemplified by Ahab's seizure of Naboth's vineyard (1 Kgs 21) and the systemic land-grabbing condemned by Isaiah (Isa 5:8). The prince must give his sons' inheritance only from his own holdings. This legislation anchors the anti-oppression principle announced in 45:8-9 in concrete property law.

heb.paragraph: The mevashshot (from b-sh-l, 'to boil, cook') are located in the four corners of the outer court, each measuring 40 by 30 cubits with rows of stonework around the base for fires. These kitchens serve a specific function: the priests boil the guilt offering (asham) and sin offering (chattat) and bake the grain offering (minchah) in them, so that they do not need to bring the holy offerings into the outer court and inadvertently 'sanctify the people' (v. 20). The sanctification concern echoes 44:19 — holy objects transfer holiness to whatever they contact, which would impose ritual obligations on unprepared laypersons.

refs.note: The sin offering regulations specify that it must be eaten in the holy place by the priests. Ezekiel's priestly kitchens provide the architectural infrastructure for this requirement.
The priests must change garments when moving from the inner court to the outer court to avoid sanctifying the people. The separate kitchen systems serve the same holiness-management purpose.
The wicked practice of Eli's sons, who took the best portions of sacrificial meat for themselves. Ezekiel's regulated kitchen system eliminates the opportunity for such priestly corruption.

notes.note: The priestly kitchens keep the most holy offerings within the inner precinct. The rationale is explicit: to prevent inadvertent sanctification of the people. Holiness is not merely a status but a contagion that requires careful management. Objects and spaces that contact the most holy become holy themselves.

The four corner kitchens in the outer court are for the people's fellowship offerings. These offerings are eaten communally, so the kitchens must be accessible to the Levitical servants who prepare them. The four-corner placement ensures equal access from all parts of the outer court. Even kitchen design serves theological function.

Calvin sees the kitchen regulations as exemplifying the principle that nothing in God's house is too mundane for divine ordering. If God regulates how sacrificial meat is boiled, how much more does He care about the disposition of human hearts in worship? The distinction between priestly and Levitical kitchens teaches that different levels of sacred responsibility require different accommodations.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that the verb 'sanctify' (qiddesh) here describes an unwanted transfer of holiness. Contact with the most holy offerings would impose ritual obligations on ordinary people who were not prepared for them. The kitchens thus function as holiness-containment infrastructure.

The NET observes that the dimensions of the corner courts (40 x 30 cubits) are identical, suggesting standardized construction. The phrase 'enclosed courts' (chatserot qeturot) uses a rare adjective that may mean 'roofed' or 'cornered,' though the exact architectural form is debated.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block highlights the pragmatic genius of the kitchen system: it solves the holiness-contagion problem identified in 44:19 through architectural design rather than behavioral regulation. Instead of relying on priests to remember to change garments every time they carry offerings, the kitchens keep the most holy offerings permanently within the inner precinct. Block calls this 'holiness by design' — the built environment enforces what human memory might fail to maintain.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli identifies this unit as a Tempelbaunotiz (temple construction note) that completes the tour of the sacred complex begun in chapter 40. The graded kitchen system reflects the same priestly theology of spatial holiness that governs the entire vision. Zimmerli notes that the detailed attention to cooking facilities is unusual in visionary literature and suggests a practical, operational orientation — the temple is not merely a symbol but a functioning institution.

hist.context: The guide leads Ezekiel through two sets of kitchens. The first set is located in the western end of the priestly chambers along the inner court, where the priests cook the guilt offering, sin offering, and grain offering. These remain within the priestly zone to prevent holy offerings from being carried into the outer court and inadvertently sanctifying the common people through contact. The second set consists of four enclosed courts in the four corners of the outer court, each 40 by 30 cubits. These are where the temple servants (Levites) boil the people's sacrifices — specifically the fellowship offerings, of which the worshiper eats a portion. The distinction between the two kitchen systems reflects the graded holiness of the offerings: the most holy offerings (sin, guilt, grain) are prepared in the inner, priestly kitchens; the lesser holy offerings (fellowship) are prepared in the outer, Levitical kitchens. This seemingly mundane architectural detail powerfully illustrates Ezekiel's central principle: holiness requires spatial organization.

rows.label: vv.1-3
rows.text: Inner East Gate: Sabbath/New Moon Opening

rows.label: vv.4-8
rows.text: Sabbath and New Moon Offering Quantities

lit.note: Chapter 46 completes the liturgical regulations by addressing worship schedules, processional order, property law, and cultic infrastructure. The range from sacrifice to kitchens demonstrates that holiness governs every detail.

themes.note: Worship dominates as the chapter details the complete liturgical calendar and processional regulations. Holiness remains high through the kitchen system's management of sacred contagion. Justice appears prominently in the anti-confiscation property law.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Prince's Worship, Land Inheritance, and Temple Kitchens

translations.text: On the day of the New Moon he is to offer a young bull, six lambs and a ram, all without defect.
On the day of the new moon he shall offer a bull from the herd without blemish, and six lambs and a ram, which shall be without blemish.
The burnt offering the prince brings to the Lord on the Sabbath day is to be six male lambs and a ram, all without defect.
The burnt offering that the prince offers to the LORD on the Sabbath day shall be six lambs without blemish and a ram without blemish.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The Prince's Worship, Land Inheritance, and Temple Kitchens within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

coaching.tip: The prince enters through the vestibule of the gate and stands by the gatepost while the priests offer his sacrifice (v. 2). He's prominent but subordinate — he doesn't offer the sacrifice himself. Ezekiel's prince is not a priest-king. The separation of civil and sacerdotal authority anticipates the New Testament distinction between Christ's offices and prevents the concentration of power that corrupted Israel's kings.

The prince cannot seize his subjects' property to give to his sons (v. 18) — he may only bequeath his own inheritance. This law directly addresses the abuse that prompted Naboth's vineyard (1 Kings 21). Ezekiel's constitution encodes protections against royal overreach. The temple vision isn't just about worship; it's a complete social charter. God's presence demands just governance because injustice desecrates the sanctuary.
